35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field $k$ and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve $C$ over $k$ (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set$ of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	$C/k$ projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	$\Hilb_{C/k}$ and its open subscheme $\Div_{C/k}$ of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective $k$-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for $\Pic$ assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map $\Div_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} $\Pic_{C/k}$. The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);
56	% arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26. Read from
57	% references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex.
58	
59	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
60	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
61	
62	The bridge from line bundles on $C \times_k T$ to the Quot scheme is the
63	Abel map: a relative effective divisor $D \subset C \times_k T$ over $T$
64	(an $S$-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
65	subscheme of $C \times_k T$ flat over $T$, hence a $T$-point of the Hilbert
66	functor $\Hilb_{C/k}(T)$, which is the special case $\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}$
67	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
68	invertible sheaf $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)$ on the relative curve,
69	representing a class in $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)$.
70	
71	\begin{lemma}[Line bundle / Quot correspondence via the Abel map]
72	  \label{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}
73	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
74	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, thm:pullback_natural}
75	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3, Def.~dfn:Abel (the Abel
76	  % map), and Thm.~th:repDiv (representability of relative effective
77	  % divisors by an open subscheme of $\IHilb_{X/S}$); arXiv:math/0504020,
78	  % pp.~25, 23 (read from
79	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1837-L1841 + L2135-L2145).
80	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
81	  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:repDiv}
82	  %   Assume $X/S$ is projective and flat.  Then $\Div_{X/S}$ is
83	  %  representable by an open subscheme $\IDiv_{X/S}$ of the Hilbert scheme
84	  %  $\IHilb_{X/S}$.
85	  %   \end{thm}"
86	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
87	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{dfn:Abel}
88	  %   The {\it Abel map\/} is the natural map of functors
89	  %      $$A_{X/S}(T)\:\Div_{X/S}(T)\to \Pic_{X/S}(T)$$
90	  %   defined by sending a relative effective divisor $D$ on $X_T/T$ to the
91	  %  sheaf $\mc O_{\!X_T}(D)$.  The target $\Pic_{X/S}$ may be replaced by any
92	  %  of its associated sheaves."
93	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3,
94	  Thm.~th:repDiv + Def.~dfn:Abel (cf.\ [Nitsure], ``Construction of Hilbert
95	  and Quot Schemes'', \S 1, where $\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}$).}
96	  Let $C/k$ be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve, and let
97	  $T$ be a $k$-scheme. There is a natural map of presheaves on $(\Sch/k)^{op}$
98	  \[
99	    A_{C/k} \;\colon\; \Div_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et},
100	    \qquad D \;\longmapsto\; [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)],
101	  \]
102	  where $\Div_{C/k}(T)$ is the set of relative effective Cartier divisors
103	  on $C \times_k T$ flat over $T$, and the target is the
104	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of
105	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}. The functor $\Div_{C/k}$ is representable
106	  by an open subscheme $\Div_{C/k} \subseteq \Hilb_{C/k}$ of the Hilbert scheme
107	  of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (with $\Hilb_{C/k}$ the special case
108	  $\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}$ of the Quot functor). The Abel map is therefore
109	  representable by a morphism of $k$-schemes $A_{C/k} : \Div_{C/k} \to
110	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$.
111	\end{lemma}
112	
113	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
114	% L1843-L1866, proof of th:repDiv)
115	% "Set $H:=\IHilb_{X/S}$, and let $W\subset X\x H$ be the universal
116	% (closed) subscheme, and $q\:W\to H$ the projection.  Let $V$ denote the
117	% set of points $w\in W$ at which $W$ is an effective divisor. Plainly $V$
118	% is open in $W$.  Set $Z:=q(W-V)$.  Then $Z$ is closed because $q$ is
119	% proper.  Set $U:=H-Z$.  Then $U$ is open, and $q^{-1}U$ is an effective
120	% divisor in $X\x U$.  In fact, since $q$ is flat, $q^{-1}U$ is a relative
121	% effective divisor in $X\x U/U$.
122	% [\dots]"
123	\begin{proof}
124	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, thm:pullback_natural}
125	  By \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, the Hilbert functor $\Hilb_{C/k} =
126	  \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}$ is representable by a $k$-scheme; this uses
127	  $C/k$ projective and flat (the smooth proper curve hypothesis is
128	  stronger). Let $W \subseteq C \times_k \Hilb_{C/k}$ be the universal
129	  closed subscheme and $q : W \to \Hilb_{C/k}$ the projection. The locus
130	  $V \subseteq W$ where $W$ is locally an effective Cartier divisor (i.e.\
131	  cut out by one regular element) is open. The complement $W \setminus V$
132	  is closed in $W$, and the image $Z := q(W \setminus V) \subseteq
133	  \Hilb_{C/k}$ is closed because $q$ is proper. Hence $\Div_{C/k} :=
134	  \Hilb_{C/k} \setminus Z$ is open in $\Hilb_{C/k}$ and represents the
135	  subfunctor of relative effective divisors: on $\Div_{C/k}$ the
136	  restriction of $W$ is flat and cut out by one regular element fiberwise,
137	  so by the standard relative-divisor criterion (Kleiman~\S 3
138	  Lem.~lm:ctn) it is a relative effective divisor on $C \times_k
139	  \Div_{C/k} / \Div_{C/k}$.
140	
141	  The Abel map sends a relative effective divisor $D \subseteq C \times_k T$
142	  to the invertible sheaf $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) := \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}$
143	  (the dual of the ideal sheaf, which is invertible by the divisor
144	  hypothesis). The assignment is functorial in $T$: pullback of an ideal
145	  sheaf along $\mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T$
146	  takes the ideal of $D$ to the ideal of $D_{T'}$ (since $D$ is flat over
147	  $T$, the schematic inverse image $D_{T'}$ is a relative effective
148	  divisor on $C \times_k T'/T'$, again by Kleiman~\S 3 Lem.~lm:ctn), hence
149	  takes $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)$ to $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T'}(D_{T'})$.
150	  Composing with the canonical projection
151	  $\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)$ of
152	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} and then with the
153	  \'etale sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} yields the
154	  natural map $A_{C/k}(T) : \Div_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)$.
155	\end{proof}

157	\section{The FGA representability theorem}
158	\label{sec:fga_pic_representability}
159	
160	We now state and outline the proof of Grothendieck's existence theorem
161	for the Picard scheme, in the specialisation needed for the Jacobian arm
162	of Route~A: $S = \Spec k$, $X = C$ a smooth proper geometrically integral
163	curve. The general theorem (Kleiman \S 4 Thm.~th:main) covers any
164	$X/S$ projective Zariski-locally over $S$, flat, with integral geometric
165	fibers; we record the general statement and then specialise.
166	
167	\begin{theorem}[FGA representability of the Picard scheme]
168	  \label{thm:fga_pic_representability}
169	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
170	  \uses{def:pic_scheme, lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence,
171	        thm:quot_representable, def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification,
172	        thm:pullback_natural}
173	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main,
174	  % and Cor.~cor:algsch (specialisation to $S = \Spec k$, $X$ complete);
175	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~26, 35 (read from
176	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2155-L2166 + L2686-L2690).
177	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
178	  % "\begin{thm}[Main]\label{th:main}
179	  %   Assume $f\:X\to S$ is projective Zariski locally over $S$, and is flat
180	  %  with integral geometric fibers.
181	  %
182	  %  \tu{(1)} Then $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists, is separated and locally of finite
183	  %  type over $S$, and represents $\Pic_{(X/S)\et}$.
184	  %
185	  %  \tu{(2)} If also $S$ is Noetherian and $X/S$ is projective, then
186	  %  $\IPic_{X/S}$ is a disjoint union of open
187	  %   subschemes, each an increasing union of open quasi-projective
188	  %  $S$-schemes.
189	  %   \end{thm}"
190	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
191	  % "\begin{sbscor}\label{cor:algsch}
192	  %    Assume $S$ is the spectrum of a field $k$, and $X$ is complete.  Then
193	  %  $\IPic_{X/k}$ exists, represents $\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}$, and is a disjoint
194	  %  union of open quasi-projective subschemes.
195	  %   \end{sbscor}"
196	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main
197	  + Cor.~cor:algsch.}
198	  Let $k$ be a field and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically integral curve
199	  over $k$. Then the \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor
200	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} is
201	  representable by a $k$-scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$, separated and locally of
202	  finite type over $k$, which is a disjoint union of open
203	  quasi-projective $k$-subschemes, each an increasing union of open
204	  quasi-projective subschemes. The scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$ is called the
205	  \emph{Picard scheme} of $C/k$.
206	\end{theorem}
207	
208	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
209	% L2168-L2366, proof of th:main; long and is broken into the four-step
210	% sketch below per the project's structural restatement convention. The
211	% verbatim quote of the opening reduction step is:)
212	% "By~\cite[(0, 4.5.5), p.~106]{EGAG}, it is a local matter on $S$ to
213	% represent a Zariski sheaf on the category of $S$-schemes.  Moreover, it
214	% is also a local matter on $S$ to prove that an $S$-scheme is separated
215	% and locally of finite type.  Hence, in order to prove (1), we may assume
216	% $S$ is Noetherian and $X/S$ is projective.
217	% [\dots]
218	% Plainly an $S$-scheme is separated if it is a disjoint union of
219	% separated open subschemes, or if it is an increasing union of
220	% separated open subschemes.  Hence (1) follows from (2)."
221	% (subsequent steps quoted inline in the project-notation proof below).
222	\begin{proof}
223	  \uses{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence, thm:quot_representable,
224	        def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification, thm:pullback_natural,
225	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
226	  We follow Kleiman~\S 4 Thm.~th:main, specialised to $S = \Spec k$ and
227	  $X = C$. The proof proceeds in four conceptual steps; the underlying
228	  algebraic engine is the Quot scheme of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
229	  and the \'etale-sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
230	  together with the line-bundle / Quot correspondence of
231	  \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}.
232	
233	  \emph{Step 1: stratify by Hilbert polynomial.}
234	  For each polynomial $\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]$, let $P^\phi \subseteq
235	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ be the \'etale subsheaf whose $T$-points are
236	  represented by invertible sheaves $\mathcal{L}$ on $C \times_k T$ with
237	  $\chi(C_t, \mathcal{L}_t^{-1}(n)) = \phi(n)$ for all $t \in T$ (the
238	  Hilbert polynomial relative to the chosen polarisation
239	  $\mathcal{O}_C(1)$ on the projective curve $C$). The $P^\phi$ form a
240	  disjoint open cover of $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (openness via Kleiman's
241	  flat-base-change argument; cf.\ EGA III 7.9.11). It suffices to
242	  represent each $P^\phi$ by an increasing union of open quasi-projective
243	  $k$-schemes.
244	
245	  \emph{Step 2: bound twists by $m$-regularity.}
246	  Given $\phi$, fix $m$ large enough that the $T$-points of $P^\phi$ are
247	  representable by $\mathcal{L}$ on $C \times_k T$ satisfying
248	  $R^i \pi_{T*} \mathcal{L}(n) = 0$ for all $i \ge 1$, $n \ge m$. Twisting
249	  by $\mathcal{O}_C(m)$ defines an automorphism of $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$
250	  that carries $P^\phi$ to $P^{\phi_0}_0$ for $\phi_0(n) := \phi(m + n)$.
251	  Hence it suffices to represent each $P^{\phi_0}_0$ by a quasi-projective
252	  $k$-scheme.
253	
254	  \emph{Step 3: factor through the Abel map.}
255	  Consider the Abel map $A_{C/k} : \Div_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$
256	  of \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}. The source $\Div_{C/k}$
257	  is representable by an open subscheme of $\Hilb_{C/k}$ (and hence by an
258	  open subscheme of the Quot scheme of \cref{thm:quot_representable}), and
259	  is quasi-projective over $k$. Pulling back along
260	  $P^{\phi_0}_0 \hookrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ yields an open
261	  subscheme $Z := P^{\phi_0}_0 \times_{\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}} \Div_{C/k}$
262	  of $\Div_{C/k}$, which is again quasi-projective. The projection
263	  $\alpha : Z \to P^{\phi_0}_0$ is a surjection of \'etale sheaves:
264	  given a $T$-point $\lambda$ of $P^{\phi_0}_0$ represented by an \'etale
265	  cover $T' \to T$ and an invertible sheaf $\mathcal{L}'$ on $C \times_k
266	  T'$ with $H^1(C_{t'}, \mathcal{L}'_{t'}) = 0$, the projective bundle
267	  $\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to T'$ associated to
268	  $\mathcal{Q} := \pi_{T'*}\mathcal{L}'$ is smooth over $T'$; an \'etale
269	  cover $T_1 \to T'$ admits a $T'$-map $T_1 \to \mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q})$
270	  (EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii)) whose composition with the universal divisor map
271	  $\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to Z$ produces the required $T_1$-point of $Z$.
272	  Moreover, the first projection $Z \times_{P^{\phi_0}_0} Z \to Z$ is
273	  smooth and proper.
274	
275	  \emph{Step 4: descend by the smooth-proper quotient lemma.}
276	  Apply Kleiman~\S 4 Lem.~lm:qt: given a surjection $\alpha : Z \to P$
277	  of \'etale sheaves with $Z$ quasi-projective, $R := Z \times_P Z$
278	  representable, and the first projection $R \to Z$ smooth and proper,
279	  $P$ is representable by a quasi-projective scheme. In our case
280	  $P = P^{\phi_0}_0$, $Z$ and $R$ as above, so $P^{\phi_0}_0$ is
281	  quasi-projective. Reassembling over $\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]$ and
282	  over $m$, the disjoint union of representable $P^\phi$ pieces represents
283	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$. The total scheme is separated (as a disjoint
284	  union of separated quasi-projectives) and locally of finite type. This
285	  is Kleiman~\S 4 Cor.~cor:algsch in the $S = \Spec k$,
286	  $X = C$ complete case.
287	\end{proof}
288	
289	\subsection{The smooth-proper quotient lemma}
290	\label{subsec:fga_pic_qt}
291	
292	The structural lemma underlying step 4 above is recorded separately
293	because it is consumed verbatim in the assembly proof: it is the
294	abstract content allowing the descent from $Z = \Div^{\phi_0}_0$ to the
295	representing Picard piece.
296	
297	\begin{lemma}[Smooth-proper quotient of \'etale sheaves]
298	  \label{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}
299	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
300	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt;
301	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~31 (read from
302	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2368-L2375).
303	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
304	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lm:qt}
305	  %   Let $\alpha\:Z\to P$ be a map of \'etale sheaves, and set $R:=Z\x_PZ$.
306	  %  Assume $\alpha$ is a surjection, $Z$ is representable by a
307	  %  quasi-projective $S$-scheme, $R$ is representable by an $S$-scheme, and
308	  %  the first projection $R\to Z$ is representable by a smooth and proper
309	  %  map.  Then $P$ is representable by a quasi-projective $S$-scheme, and
310	  %  $\alpha$ is representable by a smooth map."
311	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt.}
312	  Let $\alpha : Z \to P$ be a morphism of \'etale sheaves on $(\Sch/k)$.
313	  Set $R := Z \times_P Z$. Suppose:
314	  \begin{enumerate}
315	    \item $\alpha$ is a surjection of \'etale sheaves;
316	    \item $Z$ is representable by a quasi-projective $k$-scheme;
317	    \item $R$ is representable by a $k$-scheme;
318	    \item the first projection $R \to Z$ is representable by a smooth
319	      and proper map.
320	  \end{enumerate}
321	  Then $P$ is representable by a quasi-projective $k$-scheme, and $\alpha$
322	  is representable by a smooth map.
323	\end{lemma}
324	
325	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
326	% L2377-L2417, proof of lm:qt; reproduced verbatim because it is the
327	% structural heart of step 4.)
328	% "To lighten the notation, let $Z$ and $R$ denote the
329	% corresponding schemes as well.  Since the structure map $Z\to S$ is
330	% quasi-projective, it is separated; whence, the first projection
331	% $Z\x_SZ\to Z$ is too.  But the first projection $R\to Z$ is proper, and
332	% factors naturally through a map $h\:R\to Z\x_SZ$.  Hence $h$ is proper.
333	% But $h$ is a monomorphism; that is, $h$ is injective on $T$-points.
334	% Therefore, $h$ is a closed embedding by \cite[8.11.5]{EGAIV3}.
335	%
336	% Plainly, for each $S$-scheme $T$, the subset $R(T)\subset
337	% Z(T)\x_{S(T)}Z(T)$ is the graph of an equivalence relation.  Also, the
338	% map of schemes $R\to Z$ is flat and proper, and $Z$ is a
339	% quasi-projective $S$-scheme.  It follows that there exist a
340	% quasi-projective $S$-scheme $Q$ and a faithfully flat and projective map
341	% $Z\to Q$ such that $R=Z\x_QZ$.  (In other words, a flat and proper
342	% equivalence relation on a quasi-projective scheme is effective.)
343	% [\dots]
344	% Since $Z\to Q$ is flat, it is smooth if (and only if) its fibers are
345	% smooth.  But these fibers are, up to extension of the ground field, the
346	% same as those of $R\to Z$.  And $R\to Z$ is smooth by hypothesis.  Thus
347	% $Z\to Q$ is smooth.
348	%
349	% It remains to see that $Q$ represents $P$.  First, observe that $Z\to Q$
350	% is (represents) a surjection of \'etale sheaves.
351	% [\dots]
352	% Since $Z\to Q$ is a surjection of \'etale sheaves, $Q$ is, in the
353	% category of \'etale sheaves, the coequalizer of the pair of maps
354	% $R\rightrightarrows Z$ by Exercise~\ref{ex:epi} below, which is an
355	% elementary exercise in general Grothendieck topology.  By the same
356	% exercise, $P$ too is the coequalizer of this pair of maps.  But, in any
357	% category, the coequalizer is unique up to unique isomorphism.  Thus $Q$
358	% represents $P$."
359	\begin{proof}
360	  By (ii) the structure map $Z \to \Spec k$ is quasi-projective, hence
361	  separated, so the first projection $Z \times_k Z \to Z$ is separated.
362	  By (iv) the first projection $R \to Z$ is proper; it factors through
363	  $h : R \to Z \times_k Z$ over the diagonal embedding (the second
364	  component is the second projection of $R \to Z \times_P Z$). The map
365	  $h$ is therefore proper, and a monomorphism (because $R$ is the graph
366	  of an equivalence relation on $Z$, hence injective on $T$-points).
367	  By EGA IV 8.11.5, a proper monomorphism is a closed immersion: $h$ is a
368	  closed embedding.
369	
370	  Hence $R \hookrightarrow Z \times_k Z$ is a flat (by (iv)) and proper
371	  equivalence relation on the quasi-projective $k$-scheme $Z$. By the
372	  theory of flat-and-proper effective equivalence relations on
373	  quasi-projective schemes (Altman--Kleiman; the construction places
374	  the graph $R \subseteq Z \times_k Z$ inside the universal subscheme of
375	  the Hilbert scheme $\Hilb_{Z/k}$ and descends to a closed subscheme
376	  $Q \subseteq \Hilb_{Z/k}$ representing the quotient), there exist a
377	  quasi-projective $k$-scheme $Q$ and a faithfully flat, projective map
378	  $Z \to Q$ with $R = Z \times_Q Z$.
379	
380	  The map $Z \to Q$ is flat and proper; its fibers, up to field
381	  extension, coincide with those of $R \to Z$, which are smooth by
382	  hypothesis. Hence $Z \to Q$ is smooth.
383	
384	  Finally, $Z \to Q$ is a surjection of \'etale sheaves (an \'etale
385	  cover of any $T$-point of $Q$ pulls back to a $T'$-point of $Z$, by
386	  EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii) applied to $Z \times_Q T \to T$). In the category
387	  of \'etale sheaves, both $Q$ and $P$ are coequalisers of the pair
388	  $R \rightrightarrows Z$; the coequaliser is unique up to unique
389	  isomorphism, so $Q$ represents $P$. Smoothness of $\alpha : Z \to P$
390	  is the smoothness of $Z \to Q$ just shown.
391	\end{proof}
392	
393	\section{Group-scheme structure}
394	\label{sec:fga_pic_group_structure}
395	
396	The Picard scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$ inherits an abelian group structure from
397	the tensor product of invertible sheaves on $C \times_k T$. The relative
398	Picard presheaf $T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T$ is already an abelian
399	group at every $T$ (the quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup),
400	functorially in $T$; this lifts to the \'etale sheafification, and then
401	to a group-scheme structure on the representing scheme by Yoneda.
402	
403	\begin{theorem}[$\Pic_{C/k}$ is a $k$-group scheme]
404	  \label{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}
405	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure}
406	  \uses{def:pic_scheme, thm:fga_pic_representability,
407	        def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification, thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
408	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs (the relative
409	  % Picard functor takes values in abelian groups) and \S 4 surrounding
410	  % text (group-scheme structure on $\IPic_{X/S}$ via tensor product);
411	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~17, 26 (read from
412	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318 + L2057-L2069).
413	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
414	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
415	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
416	  %      $$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
417	  %   Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
418	  %  topologies  by
419	  %      $$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
420	  %      \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
421	  %   \end{dfn}"
422	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
423	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
424	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~\ref{df:Pfs}
425	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
426	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by $\IPic_{X/S}$.  Moreover, we say simply that the
427	  %  Picard scheme $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists."
428	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs +
429	  \S 4, Def.~df:Psch (the Picard scheme).}
430	  Let $\Pic_{C/k}$ be the Picard scheme of
431	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}. Then $\Pic_{C/k}$ is canonically an
432	  abelian group scheme over $k$: the tensor product
433	  $[\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}]$
434	  and inverse $-[\mathcal{L}] := [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]$ on
435	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)$ (well defined because both operations
436	  preserve isomorphism classes and descend through the subgroup
437	  $H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)$ of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined})
438	  are natural in $T$, hence by Yoneda are represented by morphisms
439	  $m : \Pic_{C/k} \times_k \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}$,
440	  $i : \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}$, and
441	  $e : \Spec k \to \Pic_{C/k}$ (the trivial line bundle class) satisfying
442	  the abelian-group axioms. In particular, $\Pic_{C/k}$ is a $k$-group
443	  scheme locally of finite type.
444	\end{theorem}
445	
446	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- Kleiman \S 2--4 do not isolate a
447	% separate proof block for the group-scheme structure: it is implicit in
448	% the fact that $\Pic_{(X/S)\et}$ takes values in abelian groups (df:Pfs)
449	% and that representability transfers algebraic operations from set-valued
450	% functors to scheme morphisms by Yoneda. The structural restatement below
451	% records the standard transfer.
452	\begin{proof}
453	  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability,
454	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
455	  The relative Picard functor $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$ of
456	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} is already abelian-group
457	  valued, with sum $[\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] = [\mathcal{L} \otimes
458	  \mathcal{M}]$, inverse $-[\mathcal{L}] = [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]$, and unit
459	  $0 = [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}]$; these operations descend through
460	  the quotient by $H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)$ because $\pi_T^*$ is itself a
461	  group homomorphism. The \'etale sheafification step of
462	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} preserves the abelian-group structure
463	  on values, because sheafification of an abelian-presheaf is again an
464	  abelian-sheaf (the sheafification functor commutes with finite products
465	  in the value category).
466	
467	  By \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ is
468	  represented by the scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$. The Yoneda embedding is fully
469	  faithful and preserves limits, so the natural transformations
470	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \times \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to
471	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (multiplication),
472	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (inverse), and the
473	  unit map $h_{\Spec k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ pick out unique
474	  morphisms of representing schemes $m$, $i$, $e$ as displayed. The
475	  abelian-group axioms (associativity, commutativity, identity,
476	  inverse) hold at the level of $T$-points functorially in $T$, hence
477	  hold on the representing scheme by Yoneda. The local-finite-type
478	  property is part of \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}.
479	\end{proof}
480	
481	\section{The Picard scheme: the named declaration}
482	\label{sec:fga_pic_named_definition}
483	
484	The two preceding theorems are existence statements. We record the
485	named declaration corresponding to the Lean target.
486	
487	\begin{definition}[The Picard scheme]
488	  \label{def:pic_scheme}
489	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme}
490	  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability, def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification}
491	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Def.~df:Psch;
492	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26 (read from
493	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2057-L2062).
494	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
495	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
496	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~\ref{df:Pfs}
497	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
498	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by $\IPic_{X/S}$.  Moreover, we say simply that the
499	  %  Picard scheme $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists."
500	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Def.~df:Psch.}
501	  Let $C/k$ be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field
502	  $k$. The \emph{Picard scheme} $\Pic_{C/k}$ is the $k$-scheme representing
503	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$, supplied by
504	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, equipped with the abelian-group-scheme
505	  structure of \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}. The scheme is separated,
506	  locally of finite type over $k$, and a disjoint union of open
507	  quasi-projective $k$-subschemes.
508	\end{definition}
509	
510	\section{Lean encoding}
511	\label{sec:fga_pic_lean_encoding}
512	
513	The Lean target file is the new
514	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean}, sibling to
515	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}
516	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) and
517	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean}
518	(\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}). The blueprint declarations of
519	this chapter correspond to Lean targets as follows.
520	
521	\begin{itemize}
522	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme}
523	    (\cref{def:pic_scheme}) is a structural constant naming the scheme
524	    representing $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$; it is the witness extracted
525	    from the \texttt{Functor.RepresentableBy} produced by
526	    \texttt{representable} below.
527	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
528	    (\cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}) is the morphism of
529	    $k$-schemes $\Div_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ sending a
530	    relative effective divisor to its associated invertible sheaf.
531	    The construction uses the Hilbert / Quot scheme produced by
532	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (named
533	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotScheme} per the directive's
534	    sibling-chapter naming), restricts to the open subscheme of relative
535	    effective divisors via the Kleiman~\S 3 effective-divisor open
536	    criterion, and composes with the quotient map to
537	    $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$.
538	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
539	    (\cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}) is the Altman--Kleiman
540	    descent of a flat-and-proper equivalence relation $R \subseteq Z
541	    \times_k Z$ on a quasi-projective $k$-scheme $Z$ to a
542	    quasi-projective quotient $Q$, packaged in the form that consumes
543	    a $\Functor.\mathrm{RepresentableBy}$-witness for $Z$ and produces
544	    one for the coequalising sheaf.
545	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
546	    (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}) is the assembly: from the
547	    \texttt{abelMap}, the \texttt{QuotScheme} representability witness,
548	    and the \texttt{rel\_pic\_etale\_sheafification} of
549	    \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}, build the
550	    $\Functor.\mathrm{RepresentableBy}$-witness for
551	    $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ via the four steps of the proof above
552	    (Hilbert-polynomial stratification, $m$-regularity bound, Abel-map
553	    factorisation, smooth-proper quotient).
554	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure}
555	    (\cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}) is the
556	    \texttt{CommGroupObject} (or
557	    \texttt{AddCommGrp\_Object}) instance on \texttt{PicScheme} obtained
558	    by Yoneda transport of the abelian-group structure on
559	    $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$.
560	\end{itemize}
561	
562	No new core axiom is required: this chapter is an assembly chapter on
563	top of the QuotScheme engine, the relative-Picard \'etale sheafification,
564	and the line-bundle pullback machinery already in place. The single
565	large external input (the Altman--Kleiman theorem of effective
566	flat-and-proper equivalence relations on quasi-projective schemes) is
567	recorded as \cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient} and is itself a standard
568	result; if its Lean formalisation is not yet in Mathlib, the
569	\texttt{smoothProperQuotient} Lean declaration may be sorried and the
570	gap reported to the plan agent, as it is logically downstream of the
571	Hilbert-scheme construction of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}.
572	
573	\section{Out of scope}
574	\label{sec:fga_pic_out_of_scope}
575	
576	This chapter packages \emph{only} the representable Picard scheme
577	$\Pic_{C/k}$ as an assembly of sibling-chapter inputs, plus the
578	group-scheme refinement. The following downstream constructions live in
579	separate sibling chapters and are explicitly out of scope here:
580	\begin{itemize}
581	  \item \textbf{Identity component $\Pic^0_{C/k}$} (A.3) --- the open and
582	    closed subgroup scheme of $\Pic_{C/k}$ containing the unit and
583	    consisting of those classes lying in the connected component of
584	    $\Pic_{C/k}$. Its dimension, smoothness, and abelian-variety
585	    structure are the content of the A.3 chapter
586	    \texttt{Picard\_Pic0AbelianVariety.tex} (planned), gated on this
587	    chapter.
588	  \item \textbf{Quot scheme representability} --- consumed by
589	    \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability} via \texttt{thm:quot\_representable}
590	    (\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}), not re-proved here.
591	  \item \textbf{\'Etale sheafification of $\Pic^\sharp$} ---
592	    \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c) produces
593	    $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ from the set-valued presheaf of
594	    \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}; this chapter consumes the
595	    sheaf as a black box.
596	  \item \textbf{Universal Poincar\'e sheaf} (Kleiman~\S 4 Ex.~ex:univshf)
597	    --- exists when $\Pic_{C/k}$ represents $\Pic_{C/k}$ (as opposed to
598	    its \'etale sheafification) and $C/k$ has a section; out of scope
599	    here. The Albanese / Abel--Jacobi consumption in
600	    \cref{chap:AbelJacobi} works with the \'etale-sheafified
601	    $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ representation and does not require the
602	    Poincar\'e sheaf.
603	  \item \textbf{Mumford's example} (Kleiman~\S 4 eg:Mumford), counter-examples
604	    to representability for non-integral geometric fibers --- not
605	    relevant to a smooth proper geometrically integral curve.
606	\end{itemize}
607

2	
3	\chapter{The line bundle $\mathcal O_C(P)$ and its global sections (RR.3)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6, p.~144 (definition
7	% of $\mathscr L(D)$); II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective divisors
8	% as divisors of zeros of global sections); IV.1, pp.~294--297
9	% (Riemann--Roch, Lemma~1.2, Examples~1.3.3--1.3.5)
10	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161,
11	% 174, 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks
12	% below were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images
13	% of the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
14	
15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
23	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group $\Div(C)$, the degree map $\deg \colon \Div(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z$, and the divisor $[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~$0$
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}); the global-sections
35	calculation $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ on a smooth
36	proper genus-$0$ curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve $\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1$'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(P)$ associated to a closed point $P \in C$
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its $\bar k$-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$
50	in the genus-$0$ specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that $C$ admits a non-constant rational function $f$
52	having at most a simple pole at $P$ and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	$C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ whose degree-$1$ identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*$
64	% to vanish $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ from the degree count
65	% $\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0$. Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf $\omega_X$ on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-$0$ specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$ is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-$0$ hypothesis
75	% $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0$ and the skyscraper
76	% identity $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$. The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-$g$
79	% statement.
80	
81	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, $\bar k$ is an
82	algebraically closed field and $C$ is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over $\bar k$. Concretely, the Lean signatures
84	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}) carry the same
85	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
86	\begin{quote}
87	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
88	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
89	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
90	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
91	\end{quote}
92	The genus $g(C)$ is the one defined in \Cref{def:genus} of
93	\texttt{Genus.tex}; the divisor $[P]$ of a closed point is the one of
94	\Cref{def:divisor_closed_point} of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex};
95	the principal divisor $\div(f)$ of a nonzero rational function and the
96	order $\ord_Q(f)$ at a prime divisor are the ones of
97	\Cref{def:principal_divisor} and \Cref{def:order_at_point}.
98	
99	\section{The line bundle of a closed point on a curve}
100	
101	\begin{definition}
102	
103	[Line bundle of a closed point]
104	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
105	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
106	  \uses{def:divisor_closed_point, def:codim1_cycles, def:prime_divisor,
107	        def:order_at_point}
108	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
109	  % sheaf $\mathscr L(D)$ associated to a Cartier divisor) (read from
110	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
111	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $D$ be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
112	  % $X$, represented by $\{(U_i, f_i)\}$ as above. We define a subsheaf
113	  % $\mathscr L(D)$ of the sheaf of total quotient rings $\mathscr K$ by
114	  % taking $\mathscr L(D)$ to be the sub-$\mathscr O_X$-module of
115	  % $\mathscr K$ generated by $f_i^{-1}$ on $U_i$. This is well-defined,
116	  % since $f_i/f_j$ is invertible on $U_i \cap U_j$, and $f_i^{-1}$ and
117	  % $f_j^{-1}$ generate the same $\mathscr O_X$-module. We call
118	  % $\mathscr L(D)$ the \emph{sheaf associated to $D$}."
119	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.13. \emph{Let $X$ be a scheme. Then:
120	  % (a) for any Cartier divisor $D$, $\mathscr L(D)$ is an invertible
121	  % sheaf on $X$.}"
122	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of
123	  $\mathscr L(D)$ and Proposition~6.13(a)).}
124	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$ and let $P \in C$ be a
125	  closed point. Let $\mathfrak m_P \subset \mathcal O_{C, P}$ denote the
126	  maximal ideal of the local ring at $P$; by smoothness of $C$,
127	  $\mathcal O_{C, P}$ is a discrete valuation ring and $\mathfrak m_P$
128	  is generated by any uniformizer $f_P \in \mathcal O_{C, P}$ (an
129	  element of $\mathfrak m_P \setminus \mathfrak m_P^2$). The Cartier
130	  divisor associated to the closed point $P$ is the effective
131	  Cartier divisor cut out locally by the uniformizer $f_P$ on an open
132	  affine neighbourhood of $P$, and by the unit $1$ on the complement
133	  $C \setminus \{P\}$. The \emph{line bundle of the closed point $P$},
134	  denoted $\mathcal O_C(P)$, is the locally free $\mathcal O_C$-module
135	  of rank $1$ defined by Hartshorne's construction $\mathscr L(D)$
136	  applied to this Cartier divisor: explicitly, $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is the
137	  sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module of the sheaf of total quotient rings
138	  $\mathcal K_C$ (the constant sheaf of the function field $K(C)$ on the
139	  integral scheme $C$) that is generated by $f_P^{-1} \in K(C)$ on a
140	  neighbourhood of $P$ and by $1 \in K(C)$ on the complement
141	  $C \setminus \{P\}$. Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) confirms that
142	  this subsheaf is an invertible sheaf, independently of the choice of
143	  uniformizer $f_P$ (any two uniformizers differ by a unit in
144	  $\mathcal O_{C, P}^{\times}$, so they generate the same
145	  $\mathcal O_C$-submodule of $\mathcal K_C$).
146	
147	  \paragraph{Equivalent description via the ideal sheaf.} Let
148	  $\mathcal I_P \subset \mathcal O_C$ denote the ideal sheaf of the
149	  closed point $P$ (locally generated by the uniformizer $f_P$ near
150	  $P$, and equal to $\mathcal O_C$ on the complement). Then
151	  $\mathcal I_P$ is the line bundle $\mathcal O_C(-P)$ associated to the
152	  Weil divisor $-[P]$, and $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is the
153	  $\mathcal O_C$-linear dual:
154	  \[
155	    \mathcal O_C(P) \;\cong\; \mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
156	    \mathcal O_C)
157	    \;\cong\; \mathcal I_P^{-1}.
158	  \]
159	  Both descriptions yield the same invertible sheaf; the
160	  ``subsheaf of $\mathcal K_C$'' description above is the one used in
161	  the prose, while the ``dual of the ideal sheaf'' description is the
162	  most direct entry point for the Lean construction (Mathlib's
163	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData} packages
164	  quasi-coherent ideal sheaves, from which the dual is constructed via
165	  the internal-hom on invertible sheaves).
166	
167	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
168	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint} takes a
169	  scheme $C$, a point $P : C$, and a closedness witness
170	  $\texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C)}$ (the same input shape as
171	  \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}), and returns an invertible
172	  $\mathcal O_C$-module $\mathcal O_C(P)$. The well-definedness as an
173	  invertible sheaf is part of the construction and goes through
174	  smoothness of $C$ (delivering the DVR stalk at $P$) and the standard
175	  ideal-sheaf-of-a-closed-point machinery in Mathlib's
176	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf} chapter. The chapter's
177	  consumption of the closed-point hypothesis matches \texttt{RR.1}:
178	  closed plus dimension-$1$-integral delivers $\codim_C \{P\} = 1$
179	  automatically. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
180	\end{definition}
181	
182	\section{Global sections as a Riemann--Roch space}
183	
184	\begin{lemma}
185	
186	[Global sections of $\mathcal O_C(P)$ as rational functions with a
187	controlled pole]
188	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
189	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff}
190	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:order_at_point,
191	        def:principal_divisor, def:divisor_closed_point}
192	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective
193	  % divisors linearly equivalent to $D_0$ are the divisors of zeros of
194	  % global sections of $\mathscr L(D_0)$, with the explicit
195	  % identification of those global sections with rational functions
196	  % $f$ satisfying $(f) \geq -D_0$) (read from
197	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 174).
198	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 7.7. \emph{Let $X$ be a nonsingular
199	  % projective variety over the algebraically closed field $k$. Let
200	  % $D_0$ be a divisor on $X$ and let $\mathscr L = \mathscr L(D_0)$
201	  % be the corresponding invertible sheaf. Then:
202	  % (a) for each nonzero $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)$, the divisor of
203	  % zeros $(s)_0$ is an effective divisor linearly equivalent to $D_0$;
204	  % (b) every effective divisor linearly equivalent to $D_0$ is $(s)_0$
205	  % for some $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)$; and
206	  % (c) two sections $s, s' \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)$ have the same
207	  % divisor of zeros if and only if there is a $\lambda \in k^*$ such
208	  % that $s' = \lambda s$.}"
209	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(b) If $D \geq 0$ and $D = D_0 + (f)$, then
210	  % $(f) \geq -D_0$. Thus $f$ gives a global section of
211	  % $\mathscr L(D_0)$ whose divisor of zeros is $D$."
212	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.7, Proposition~7.7 and its
213	  proof of part (b), p.~157.}
214	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$, let $P \in C$ be a
215	  closed point, and let $\mathcal O_C(P)$ be the line bundle of
216	  \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}. The $\bar k$-vector space of
217	  global sections of $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is naturally identified with the
218	  Riemann--Roch space
219	  \[
220	    L([P]) \;:=\;
221	    \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0
222	    \text{ in } \Div(C)\bigr\}
223	    \,\cup\, \{0\}.
224	  \]
225	  Equivalently, $f \in K(C)^{\times}$ defines a global section of
226	  $\mathcal O_C(P)$ if and only if
227	  \[
228	    \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; 0 \quad \text{for every closed point } Q \in C
229	    \text{ with } Q \neq P,
230	    \qquad
231	    \ord_P(f) \;\geq\; -1.
232	  \]
233	  Under this identification, the inclusion
234	  $\bar k \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ of constant functions
235	  corresponds to the natural map $\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow
236	  \mathcal O_C(P)$ exhibiting $\mathcal O_C(P)$ as the twist of the
237	  structure sheaf along the prime divisor $[P]$.
238	\end{lemma}
239	
240	\begin{proof}
241	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:order_at_point,
242	        def:principal_divisor, def:divisor_closed_point}
243	  By construction, $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is the sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module of
244	  the sheaf of total quotient rings $\mathcal K_C$ generated locally by
245	  $f_P^{-1}$ near $P$ and by $1$ on $C \setminus \{P\}$, where $f_P$ is
246	  a uniformizer at $P$ (\Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}). Since $C$
247	  is integral, $\mathcal K_C$ is the constant sheaf at the function field
248	  $K(C)$, and a global section of any sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module
249	  $\mathcal F \subset \mathcal K_C$ is precisely an element of $K(C)$
250	  whose germ at every point lies in $\mathcal F$ at that point.
251	  Therefore $f \in K(C)$ is a global section of $\mathcal O_C(P)$ if
252	  and only if:
253	
254	  \begin{itemize}
255	    \item For every closed point $Q \in C$ with $Q \neq P$, the germ of
256	      $f$ at $Q$ lies in $\mathcal O_C(P)_Q = \mathcal O_{C, Q}$
257	      (the structure sheaf, since $\mathcal O_C(P)$ agrees with
258	      $\mathcal O_C$ on $C \setminus \{P\}$); equivalently,
259	      $\ord_Q(f) \geq 0$.
260	    \item At the closed point $P$, the germ of $f$ at $P$ lies in
261	      $\mathcal O_C(P)_P = f_P^{-1} \cdot \mathcal O_{C, P}$;
262	      equivalently, $f_P \cdot f \in \mathcal O_{C, P}$, i.e.\
263	      $\ord_P(f_P \cdot f) \geq 0$, i.e.\ $\ord_P(f) \geq -1$.
264	  \end{itemize}
265	  In divisorial language (\Cref{def:principal_divisor},
266	  \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}), the two conditions combine to
267	  $\div(f) + [P] \geq 0$, recovering the Riemann--Roch space description
268	  $L([P])$. The zero element of $K(C)$ is included by convention as the
269	  zero global section.
270	
271	  The identification with Hartshorne's Proposition~7.7(b) is direct:
272	  taking $D_0 = [P]$ effective, Hartshorne's argument shows that a
273	  rational function $f$ with $(f) \geq -D_0$ defines a global section
274	  of $\mathscr L(D_0)$. The condition $(f) \geq -D_0 = -[P]$ is exactly
275	  $\div(f) + [P] \geq 0$. The compatibility with the constant inclusion
276	  follows by taking $f = 1 \in K(C)^{\times}$: then $\div(1) = 0$, so
277	  $\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0$, and the corresponding section of
278	  $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is the image of $1 \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
279	  \cong \bar k$ under the structural inclusion $\mathcal O_C
280	  \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(P)$.
281	\end{proof}

283	\section{Cohomological vanishing in genus zero}
284	
285	\begin{lemma}
286	
287	[Vanishing of $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ on a genus-$0$ curve]
288	  \label{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
289	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero}
290	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:divisor_closed_point,
291	        def:genus, def:Scheme_HModule}
292	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the proof of Theorem~1.3 (the inductive
293	  % step ``Next, let $D$ be any divisor, and let $P$ be any point.''):
294	  % the short exact sequence $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to
295	  % k(P) \to 0$ and its tensor with $\mathscr L(D + P)$ giving
296	  % $0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0$
297	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
298	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider $P$ as a closed subscheme of $X$. Its
299	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf $k$ sitting at the point $P$,
300	  % which we denote by $k(P)$, and its ideal sheaf is $\mathscr L(-P)$
301	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
302	  % $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0$.
303	  % Tensoring with $\mathscr L(D + P)$ we get
304	  % $0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0$,
305	  % (since $\mathscr L(P)$ is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
306	  % does not affect the sheaf $k(P)$)."
307	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
308	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
309	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
310	  $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$, and let $P \in C$ be a closed point. Then
311	  \[
312	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 0,
313	  \]
314	  i.e.\ $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$.
315	\end{lemma}
316	
317	\begin{proof}
318	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:divisor_closed_point,
319	        def:genus, def:Scheme_HModule}
320	  Specialise Hartshorne's inductive step at $D = 0$. The standard short
321	  exact sequence
322	  \[
323	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
324	  \]
325	  (Hartshorne~II.6.18: the ideal sheaf of the locally principal closed
326	  subscheme $P$ is $\mathcal O_C(-[P])$; the quotient is the structure
327	  sheaf of $P$, which is the skyscraper sheaf $k(P) \cong \bar k$
328	  supported at $P$ since $P$ has residue field $\bar k$ by closedness
329	  over the algebraically closed base $\bar k$). Tensoring with the
330	  locally free rank-$1$ sheaf $\mathcal O_C([P])$
331	  (\Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}) preserves exactness and leaves
332	  the skyscraper sheaf $k(P)$ unchanged, giving
333	  \[
334	    0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
335	  \]
336	  in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$. Apply the long exact sequence of sheaf
337	  cohomology (\Cref{def:Scheme_HModule}, the project's $\Module \bar
338	  k$-flavoured cohomology pipeline). Using that $\dim C = 1$ kills
339	  $H^i$ for $i \geq 2$ (Grothendieck vanishing on a $1$-dimensional
340	  scheme), the long exact sequence reads
341	  \begin{multline*}
342	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^0(C, k(P)) \\
343	    \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^1(C, k(P)) \to 0.
344	  \end{multline*}
345	  Two ingredients close the proof:
346	
347	  \emph{(a) $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$.} This is the genus-$0$
348	  hypothesis: by \Cref{def:genus},
349	  \[
350	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C),
351	  \]
352	  so $g(C) = 0$ is exactly $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$,
353	  i.e.\ $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$ as a $\bar k$-vector space.
354	
355	  \emph{(b) $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$.} The skyscraper sheaf $k(P)$ is
356	  supported at a single closed point, hence is flasque: every section
357	  on an open $U \subset C$ either restricts to $0$ (if $P \notin U$) or
358	  takes values in the stalk $k(P)_P \cong \bar k$ (if $P \in U$), and
359	  restriction maps between opens of either type are surjective. A
360	  flasque sheaf on any topological space has vanishing higher
361	  cohomology (Hartshorne~III.2.5); in particular
362	  $H^i(C, k(P)) = 0$ for $i \geq 1$, and \emph{a fortiori}
363	  $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$.
364	
365	  Substituting (a) and (b) into the long exact sequence collapses the
366	  segment ending at $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ to
367	  \[
368	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to 0,
369	  \]
370	  whence $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$, as required.
371	
372	  \emph{Lean reference note on the long exact sequence.} Mathlib
373	  (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the long exact sequence of
374	  $\Ext_{\mathcal O_C}^*(\mathbf 1, -)$ at the abelian-group level
375	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact}); the
376	  project's $\Module \bar k$-flavoured cohomology pipeline
377	  (\Cref{def:Scheme_HModule}) inherits the long exact sequence by
378	  forget-functor naturality. The skyscraper-cohomology vanishing
379	  $H^i(C, k(P)) = 0$ for $i \geq 1$ is closed by either the flasque
380	  argument (Hartshorne~III.2.5; Mathlib has the abelian-sheaf form
381	  via \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.Flasque}) or, more directly for the
382	  curve case, by the affine-cover Čech-cohomology argument on
383	  $C \setminus \{P\}$ and a small affine open around $P$ (the
384	  skyscraper has zero sections on $C \setminus \{P\}$, so the Čech
385	  complex degenerates).
386	\end{proof}
387	
388	\section{The dimension formula in genus zero}
389	
390	\begin{theorem}
391	
392	[$\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ on a genus-$0$ curve]
393	  \label{thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
394	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
395	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:divisor_closed_point,
396	        def:divisor_degree, def:genus,
397	        def:eulerChar_curve,
398	        thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus,
399	        lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
400	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the Riemann--Roch chain inside
401	  % Example~1.3.5 applied with $D = [P]$: $\chi(\mathscr L([P]))
402	  % = \deg([P]) + 1 - g = 1 + 1 - 0 = 2$, combined with the vanishing
403	  % $H^1(\mathscr L([P])) = 0$ (read from
404	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 312, 314).
405	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let $D$ be a
406	  % divisor on a curve $X$ of genus $g$. Then}
407	  % $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$."
408	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any $D$,
409	  % $\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g$,
410	  % where for any coherent sheaf $\mathscr F$ on $X$, $\chi(\mathscr F)$
411	  % is the Euler characteristic
412	  % $\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)$."
413	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295 (the
414	  $\chi$-identity inside the proof) and Example~1.3.5, p.~297 (the
415	  genus-$0$ specialisation chain).}
416	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
417	  $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$, and let $P \in C$ be a closed point. Then
418	  \[
419	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 2.
420	  \]
421	\end{theorem}
422	
423	\begin{proof}
424	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:divisor_closed_point,
425	        def:divisor_degree, def:genus,
426	        def:eulerChar_curve,
427	        thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus,
428	        lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
429	  Specialise the Euler-characteristic identity
430	  \Cref{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus} of \texttt{RR.2} to
431	  the divisor $D = [P]$ of \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}. The
432	  degree of $[P]$ is $\deg([P]) = 1$ (every closed point contributes
433	  $1$ to the bare-sum degree of \Cref{def:divisor_degree}), and the
434	  genus hypothesis $g(C) = 0$ (\Cref{def:genus}) gives
435	  \[
436	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; \deg([P]) + 1 - g(C)
437	    \;=\; 1 + 1 - 0 \;=\; 2.
438	  \]
439	  Unfolding $\chi$ via \Cref{def:eulerChar_curve} as a two-term Euler
440	  characteristic on a curve, this rewrites as
441	  \[
442	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
443	    \,-\, \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
444	    \;=\; 2.
445	  \]
446	  By
447	  \Cref{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}, the second
448	  term vanishes:
449	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$. Substituting yields
450	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$, as required.
451	
452	  \emph{Alternative direct route via the long exact sequence.} The
453	  same long exact sequence used in the proof of
454	  \Cref{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero} computes
455	  the dimension of $H^0$ in one stroke without invoking
456	  \texttt{RR.2}: from
457	  \[
458	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
459	    \to H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to \cdots
460	  \]
461	  together with $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1$
462	  (Hartshorne~I.3.4: $H^0$ of the structure sheaf on a proper
463	  geometrically irreducible variety over $\bar k$ is $\bar k$),
464	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1$ (skyscraper at a $\bar k$-rational
465	  point), and $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$ (genus-$0$), the connecting
466	  map $H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ has zero codomain and the
467	  segment yields the short exact sequence
468	  \[
469	    0 \to \bar k \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to \bar k \to 0
470	  \]
471	  of $\bar k$-vector spaces, whence $\dim_{\bar k}
472	  H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 1 + 1 = 2$. This alternative route does
473	  not consume the Euler-characteristic identity of \texttt{RR.2} and
474	  could be substituted at the formalisation stage; the chapter uses
475	  \texttt{RR.2} as written above to keep the dependency graph in
476	  line with the iter-173 RR sub-build sequencing.
477	\end{proof}
478	
479	\section{Existence of a non-constant rational function}
480	
481	\begin{corollary}
482	
483	[A genus-$0$ curve admits a non-constant rational function with a
484	single simple pole]
485	  \label{cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
486	  % Alias label consumed under the shorter name by
487	  % \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex} (RR.4):
488	  \label{cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero}
489	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
490	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint,
491	        def:divisor_closed_point, def:order_at_point,
492	        def:principal_divisor, def:genus,
493	        thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero,
494	        lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
495	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Exercise~1.1 (the existence of a
496	  % nonconstant rational function regular everywhere except at one
497	  % point on a genus-$0$ curve, as a direct consequence of the
498	  % Riemann--Roch dimension formula) (read from
499	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
500	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "1.1. Let $X$ be a curve, and let $P \in X$ be a
501	  % point. Then there exists a nonconstant rational function $f \in
502	  % K(X)$, which is regular everywhere except at $P$."
503	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Exercise~1.1, p.~297.}
504	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
505	  $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$, and let $P \in C$ be a closed point. Then
506	  there exists a non-constant rational function $f \in K(C)$ such that:
507	  \begin{itemize}
508	    \item $f$ is regular on $C \setminus \{P\}$, i.e.\ $\ord_Q(f) \geq 0$
509	      for every closed point $Q \in C$ with $Q \neq P$;
510	    \item $f$ has at most a simple pole at $P$, i.e.\
511	      $\ord_P(f) \geq -1$;
512	    \item $f \notin \bar k$, equivalently
513	      $\div(f) \neq 0 \in \Div(C)$.
514	  \end{itemize}
515	  In particular, the pair $(1, f)$ spans
516	  $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ as a $\bar k$-vector space.
517	\end{corollary}
518	
519	\begin{proof}
520	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint,
521	        def:divisor_closed_point, def:order_at_point,
522	        def:principal_divisor, def:genus,
523	        thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero,
524	        lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
525	  By
526	  \Cref{thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero},
527	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$. The constant subspace
528	  $\bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$, obtained from the
529	  structural inclusion $\bar k \cong H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
530	  \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ (the rational function
531	  $f = 1$, with $\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0$, under the
532	  identification of
533	  \Cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}), is a one-dimensional
534	  $\bar k$-vector subspace. Since the ambient space has dimension $2$,
535	  the quotient $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) / \bar k \cdot 1$ is a
536	  non-zero one-dimensional $\bar k$-vector space; any lift to
537	  $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ of a non-zero element of the quotient is
538	  an element of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k \cdot 1$.
539	
540	  Choose such a lift $s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k
541	  \cdot 1$. Under the identification of
542	  \Cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff} the section
543	  $s$ corresponds to a rational function $f \in K(C)^{\times}$
544	  satisfying $\div(f) + [P] \geq 0$, equivalently $\ord_Q(f) \geq 0$
545	  for every $Q \neq P$ and $\ord_P(f) \geq -1$. The choice
546	  $s \notin \bar k \cdot 1$ means $f$ is not the image of a constant,
547	  so $f \notin \bar k$ (every non-zero element of
548	  $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \cong \bar k$ corresponds, under the
549	  identification, to a constant element of $K(C)$). The three bullets
550	  of the statement are now immediate.
551	
552	  Spanning. Linear independence of $1$ and $f$ over $\bar k$ in
553	  $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ is the same condition as
554	  $s \notin \bar k \cdot 1$ above; combined with the dimension count
555	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$, the pair $(1, f)$ is a
556	  $\bar k$-basis of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$.
557	
558	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean signature
559	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists\_nonconstant\_genusZero}
560	  returns an element $f$ of the project's function-field carrier
561	  $K(C)$ together with proofs of regularity on $C \setminus \{P\}$ and
562	  the simple-pole bound at $P$, packaged in the same shape as
563	  \Cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}'s output. The
564	  non-constancy is recorded as a separate field; the spanning
565	  statement is downstream and is consumed by \texttt{RR.4} via
566	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}.
567	\end{proof}

569	\section{Mathlib-status flag: the H$^1$-vanishing input}
570	
571	The vanishing $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$ on a genus-$0$ curve
572	(\Cref{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}) is the
573	critical cohomological input of this chapter. Its closure depends on
574	two Mathlib-status facts, both of which the project already consumes
575	elsewhere:
576	
577	\begin{itemize}
578	  \item \textbf{Long exact sequence of $\Module \bar k$-flavoured sheaf
579	    cohomology.} Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships
580	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact} for
581	    $\Ext^*$-long-exact-sequences at the abelian-group level. The
582	    project's $\Module \bar k$-flavoured pipeline
583	    (\Cref{def:Scheme_HModule}, \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex})
584	    inherits it by forget-functor naturality. This bridge is already
585	    consumed by \Cref{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus} of
586	    \texttt{RR.2}, so closing it here introduces no new Mathlib
587	    obligation.
588	  \item \textbf{Vanishing of higher cohomology of a skyscraper sheaf}
589	    on a Noetherian scheme. Hartshorne~III.2.5 gives the flasque
590	    formulation; the project's affine-cover Čech route
591	    (\Cref{def:Scheme_cechCochain_OC} and surrounding
592	    \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex} machinery) computes
593	    the same vanishing for the skyscraper at a closed point by direct
594	    Čech-degeneration. \texttt{RR.2}'s inductive step already invokes
595	    $\chi(k(P)) = 1$, which is the combined statement
596	    $\dim H^0(C, k(P)) = 1$ and $\dim H^i(C, k(P)) = 0$ for $i \geq 1$,
597	    so the vanishing $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$ is already a consumer
598	    obligation of \texttt{RR.2}.
599	\end{itemize}
600	
601	Consequently the H$^1$-vanishing
602	\Cref{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero} introduces no
603	Mathlib obligation that is not already on \texttt{RR.2}'s critical
604	path. The genus-$0$ specialisation should therefore close at the
605	formalisation stage without splitting this chapter or pushing the
606	vanishing back into \texttt{RR.2}.

1	\chapter{Abelian-variety rigidity: morphisms from a genus-$0$ curve into an abelian variety are constant}
2	\label{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/BareScheme.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/ChartIso.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/Points.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/GmScaling.lean AlgebraicJacobian/RigidityLemma.lean
4
