3	\chapter{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero (RR.2)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, IV \S1, pp.~294--297
7	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages
8	% 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below
9	% were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images of
10	% the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
11	
12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter is the second of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
15	that close the project's headline RR bridge
16	\[
17	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
18	  \,\colon\,
19	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
20	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
21	\]
22	The bridge consumed by
23	\Cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} of
24	\texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} is the Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain:
25	the Weil-divisor group $\Div(C)$ and the degree map $\deg \colon \Div(C)
26	\to \mathbb Z$ (\texttt{RR.1}, \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}); the
27	Riemann--Roch dimension formula in genus~$0$
28	\[
29	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
30	\]
31	(this chapter, \texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections identification
32	$\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ (\texttt{RR.3}); the
33	``rational curve $\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1$'' classification via
34	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
35	
36	% NOTE (Serre duality vs. direct $\chi$ — iter-173 sub-phase choice).
37	% This chapter proves the RR formula via the \emph{direct $\chi$
38	% inductive proof} (Hartshorne IV.1.3 step ``Next, let $D$ be any
39	% divisor\ldots'', p.~296), \emph{not} via Serre duality. The reason is
40	% strictly Mathlib-side: Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships no
41	% dualizing sheaf $\omega_X$ on a scheme and no Serre duality theorem
42	% $H^i(X, \mathcal F) \cong H^{n-i}(X, \omega_X \otimes \mathcal F^\vee)^*$;
43	% routing through that machinery would force a multi-iter sub-build
44	% strictly heavier than the genus-$0$ critical path requires. The
45	% direct $\chi$ proof depends only on additivity of the Euler
46	% characteristic on a short exact sequence of coherent sheaves (a
47	% standard preadditive-category fact), the genus base case
48	% $\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g$, and the closed-point skyscraper
49	% identity $\chi(k(P)) = 1$.
50	
51	% NOTE (Genus-$0$ carve-out vs. general $g$ — iter-173 sub-phase
52	% choice). The chapter restricts the headline theorem
53	% \Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} to the genus-$0$ specialisation
54	% $\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1$ for $\deg(D) \geq 0$. The general-$g$
55	% statement $\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g$ is downstream of
56	% \texttt{RR.2} (it requires the canonical divisor $K$ and Serre
57	% duality / the $K - D$ pairing) and is \emph{not} needed for the
58	% genus-$0$ critical path: \texttt{RR.3} consumes only
59	% \Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}, and \texttt{RR.4} consumes
60	% \texttt{RR.3}. The intermediate $\chi$-identity
61	% \Cref{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus} is stated and
62	% sketched for general $g$ (the inductive argument is genus-uniform);
63	% its full closure on a general-$g$ curve waits for the project's
64	% subsequent Riemann--Roch chapter, but the proof body in
65	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} pins the
66	% \emph{statement} for general $g$ and may close the body initially
67	% under a $g = 0$ hypothesis.
68	
69	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, $\bar k$ is an
70	algebraically closed field and $C$ is a smooth proper geometrically
71	irreducible curve over $\bar k$. Concretely, the Lean signatures
72	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean}) carry the same
73	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
74	\begin{quote}
75	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
76	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
77	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
78	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
79	\end{quote}
80	The genus $g(C)$ is the one defined in \Cref{def:genus} of
81	\texttt{Genus.tex}, namely $g(C) = \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$
82	via the project's $\Module\bar k$-flavoured sheaf cohomology
83	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}).
84	
85	\section{The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve}
86	
87	\begin{definition}
88	
89	
90	\leanok
91	[Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve]
92	  \label{def:eulerChar_curve}
93	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic}
94	  \uses{def:genus}
95	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of $\chi(\mathcal F)$
96	  % inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
97	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
98	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any $D$,
99	  % $\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g$,
100	  % where for any coherent sheaf $\mathscr F$ on $X$, $\chi(\mathscr F)$
101	  % is the Euler characteristic
102	  % $\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)$."
103	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295, the formula displayed
104	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3.}
105	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$ and let $\mathcal F$
106	  be a coherent sheaf on $C$. The \emph{Euler characteristic} of
107	  $\mathcal F$ is the integer
108	  \[
109	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;:=\;
110	    \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \,\dim_{\bar k} H^i(C, \mathcal F)
111	    \;=\;
112	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal F)
113	    \,-\,
114	    \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal F).
115	  \]
116	  The second equality is a curve-specific simplification: because
117	  $\dim C = 1$, Grothendieck vanishing (Hartshorne~III.2.7) gives
118	  $H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0$ for $i \geq 2$, so the alternating sum has
119	  only two terms. Coherence of $\mathcal F$ on the proper $\bar
120	  k$-scheme $C$ guarantees that both $H^0$ and $H^1$ are
121	  finite-dimensional $\bar k$-vector spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology
122	  finiteness theorem, the same finiteness backing
123	  \Cref{def:genus}), so the two terms are honest natural numbers and
124	  the difference is a well-defined integer.
125	
126	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
127	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic} takes a coherent
128	  sheaf $\mathcal F$ on $C$ and returns an integer; the natural input
129	  shape is the project's $\Module \bar k$-valued sheaf-cohomology
130	  wrapper $\HModule \bar k$ (\Cref{def:Scheme_HModule}) applied to a
131	  $\Module \bar k$-flavoured carrier built from $\mathcal F$, with
132	  $\dim_{\bar k}$ extracted via $\Module.\mathtt{finrank}$. The
133	  declaration is \texttt{noncomputable} (inherited from
134	  $\Module.\mathtt{finrank}$ and from the $\Ext$-based cohomology
135	  construction).
136	\end{definition}

138	\section{The $\ell$-invariant of a Weil divisor}
139	
140	\begin{definition}
141	
142	
143	\leanok
144	[$\ell$-invariant of a Weil divisor]
145	  \label{def:l_invariant}
146	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l}
147	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:eulerChar_curve}
148	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of $l(D)$ and of the
149	  % "dimension of $|D|$") (read from
150	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
151	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We denote $\dim_k H^0(X, \mathscr L(D))$ by $l(D)$,
152	  % so that the \emph{dimension of $|D|$} is $l(D) - 1$. The number
153	  % $l(D)$ is finite by (II, 5.19) or (III, 5.2)."
154	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295 (definition of $l(D)$).}
155	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$ and let
156	  $D \in \Div(C)$ be a Weil divisor (\Cref{def:codim1_cycles}). The
157	  \emph{$\ell$-invariant of $D$} is the integer
158	  \[
159	    \ell(D) \;:=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
160	    \;\in\; \mathbb N,
161	  \]
162	  the $\bar k$-dimension of the space of global sections of the
163	  invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$ associated to $D$. Finiteness of
164	  $\ell(D)$ is a consequence of the coherent-cohomology finiteness
165	  theorem on a proper $\bar k$-scheme (Hartshorne~II.5.19 or
166	  III.5.2). The classical \emph{complete linear system $|D|$} has
167	  $\bar k$-dimension $\ell(D) - 1$ as a projective space, but the
168	  chapter never uses the projective-space interpretation; $\ell(D)$ is
169	  the only quantity it needs.
170	
171	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
172	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l} accepts a divisor
173	  $D : C.\mathtt{WeilDivisor}$ (\Cref{def:codim1_cycles}) and returns
174	  $\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\;(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D)))$,
175	  computed through the same $\Module \bar k$-valued sheaf-cohomology
176	  pipeline as \Cref{def:eulerChar_curve}. The associated invertible
177	  sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$ is the line bundle of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
178	  chapter, to be added); the present chapter's prose and Lean
179	  signature reference $\mathcal O_C(D)$ by name and consume only its
180	  $H^0$ functor. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
181	\end{definition}

183	\section{The $\chi$ identity: $\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g$}
184	
185	\begin{theorem}
186	
187	
188	\leanok
189	[Euler-characteristic identity for $\mathcal O_C(D)$]
190	  \label{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus}
191	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus}
192	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, def:divisor_degree, def:genus,
193	        thm:divisor_degree_hom}
194	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Theorem~1.3 (the reduction inside the
195	  % proof: ``Thus we have to show that for any $D$, $\chi(\mathscr L(D))
196	  % = \deg D + 1 - g$'') (read from
197	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
198	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let $D$ be a
199	  % divisor on a curve $X$ of genus $g$. Then}
200	  % $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$."
201	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any $D$,
202	  % $\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g$,
203	  % where for any coherent sheaf $\mathscr F$ on $X$, $\chi(\mathscr F)$
204	  % is the Euler characteristic
205	  % $\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)$."
206	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295.}
207	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
208	  $\bar k$ of genus $g = g(C)$, and let $D \in \Div(C)$. Then
209	  \[
210	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g.
211	  \]
212	\end{theorem}
213	
214	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "First we consider the case $D = 0$. Then our
215	% formula says
216	% $\dim H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = 1 - g$,
217	% because $H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) = k$ for any projective variety
218	% (I, 3.4), and $\dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = g$ by (1.1).
219	% Next, let $D$ be any divisor, and let $P$ be any point. We will show
220	% that the formula is true for $D$ if and only if it is true for
221	% $D + P$. Since any divisor can be reached from $0$ in a finite number
222	% of steps by adding or subtracting a point each time, this will show
223	% the result holds for all $D$.
224	% We consider $P$ as a closed subscheme of $X$. Its structure sheaf is
225	% the skyscraper sheaf $k$ sitting at the point $P$, which we denote by
226	% $k(P)$, and its ideal sheaf is $\mathscr L(-P)$ by (II, 6.18).
227	% Therefore we have an exact sequence
228	% $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0$.
229	% Tensoring with $\mathscr L(D + P)$ we get
230	% $0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0$,
231	% (since $\mathscr L(P)$ is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
232	% does not affect the sheaf $k(P)$). Now the Euler characteristic is
233	% additive on short exact sequences (III, Ex. 5.1), and $\chi(k(P))
234	% = 1$, so we have
235	% $\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1$.
236	% On the other hand, $\deg D + P = \deg D + 1$, so our formula is true
237	% for $D$ if and only if it is true for $D + P$, as required."
238	\begin{proof}
239	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, def:divisor_degree, def:genus,
240	        thm:divisor_degree_hom, def:divisor_closed_point}
241	  The proof is by induction on the structure of $\Div(C)$ as the free
242	  abelian group on closed points (\Cref{def:codim1_cycles},
243	  \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}). Both sides of the asserted
244	  equation are additive in $D$ (the right-hand side by linearity of
245	  $\deg$, \Cref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}; the left-hand side by the
246	  inductive step proved below), so it suffices to verify the formula
247	  for $D = 0$ and to show that it transports across the elementary
248	  modification $D \mapsto D + [P]$ at any closed point $P \in C$.
249	
250	  \emph{Base case $D = 0$.} The line bundle $\mathcal O_C(0)$ is the
251	  structure sheaf $\mathcal O_C$, and the formula
252	  $\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g$ reads
253	  \[
254	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
255	    - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)
256	    \;=\; 1 - g.
257	  \]
258	  The second term is the definition of the genus,
259	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g$ (\Cref{def:genus}). The
260	  first term is $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1$: $C$ is a
261	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over the algebraically
262	  closed field $\bar k$, so its connected components agree with its
263	  geometrically connected components, and $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
264	  \cong \bar k$ as $\bar k$-vector spaces (the Hartshorne~I.3.4
265	  statement that ``$H^0(X, \mathcal O_X) = k$ for any projective
266	  variety'' over an algebraically closed field; the Mathlib home is
267	  the project's $H^0$-bridge in
268	  \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}). Substituting these
269	  two values gives $1 - g = 1 - g$, as required.
270	
271	  \emph{Inductive step (Hartshorne's ``$D \leftrightsquigarrow D + P$''
272	  step).} Let $P \in C$ be a closed point. We follow Hartshorne's
273	  argument: view $P$ as a closed subscheme of $C$; its structure
274	  sheaf is the skyscraper sheaf $k(P) \cong \bar k$ supported at $P$,
275	  and its sheaf of ideals is $\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C$
276	  (the locally principal ideal sheaf cutting out $P$ in $C$). Hence
277	  the short exact sequence
278	  \[
279	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
280	  \]
281	  in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$ (Hartshorne~II.6.18). Tensoring with the
282	  invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ (a locally free
283	  $\mathcal O_C$-module of rank $1$, so tensoring preserves exactness
284	  and leaves the skyscraper sheaf $k(P)$ unchanged) yields
285	  \[
286	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(D)
287	    \to \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
288	    \to k(P) \to 0.
289	  \]
290	  Additivity of the Euler characteristic on a short exact sequence
291	  of coherent sheaves (Mathlib's preadditive-category Euler
292	  characteristic; see the Lean reference note at the end of this
293	  proof) gives
294	  \[
295	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P]))
296	    \;=\;
297	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + \chi(k(P)).
298	  \]
299	  The skyscraper sheaf $k(P)$ has
300	  $H^0(C, k(P)) \cong \bar k$ and $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$ (the cohomology
301	  of a flasque sheaf supported at a closed point is concentrated in
302	  degree $0$, with $H^0$ equal to the stalk $\bar k$), so
303	  $\chi(k(P)) = 1$. Therefore
304	  \[
305	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + 1.
306	  \]
307	  On the other hand, $\deg([P]) = 1$ (every closed point contributes
308	  $1$ to the bare-sum degree of \Cref{def:divisor_degree}), so by
309	  \Cref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}
310	  \[
311	    \deg(D + [P]) + 1 - g \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g + 1.
312	  \]
313	  Comparing the two displayed identities, the formula
314	  $\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g$ holds for $D$ if and only
315	  if it holds for $D + [P]$. Since the closed-point divisors $[P]$
316	  generate $\Div(C)$ as a free abelian group, the formula propagates
317	  from $D = 0$ to every $D \in \Div(C)$ by induction on the (signed)
318	  number of generators in the expression of $D$.
319	
320	  \emph{Lean reference note on $\chi$-additivity.} Mathlib (snapshot
321	  \texttt{b80f227}) provides the Euler-characteristic API
322	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.EulerCharacteristic}
323	  (\texttt{HomologicalComplex.eulerChar}, \texttt{GradedObject.eulerChar})
324	  but does \emph{not} ship the specific additivity statement
325	  \texttt{CategoryTheory.ShortExact.eulerChar\_additive} [expected,
326	  not in Mathlib under that name]. The genus-$0$ proof of
327	  \Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} therefore threads additivity of
328	  $\chi$ on a short exact sequence either as a project-side lemma
329	  proved inside \texttt{RRFormula.lean} (from
330	  \texttt{Submodule.finrank\_quotient\_add\_finrank} combined with
331	  the long exact sequence of $H^*$) or as a candidate Mathlib upstream
332	  PR. The base case $\chi(k(P)) = 1$ is closed by the Mathlib
333	  skyscraper-sheaf API together with the project's $H^0$-bridge
334	  (\Cref{def:Scheme_HModule}).
335	\end{proof}

337	\section{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero}
338	
339	\begin{theorem}
340	
341	
342	\leanok
343	[Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero]
344	  \label{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}
345	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero}
346	  \uses{def:l_invariant, def:divisor_degree, def:genus,
347	        thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus,
348	        thm:principal_deg_zero}
349	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Example~1.3.5 (genus-$0$ specialisation
350	  % of Riemann--Roch; the curve-is-rational consequence) (read from
351	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
352	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is \emph{rational}
353	  % if it is birational to $\mathbf P^1$ (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in
354	  % this chapter are complete and nonsingular by definition, a curve
355	  % $X$ is rational if and only if $X \cong \mathbf P^1$ (I, 6.12). Now
356	  % using (1.3) we can show that $X$ is rational if and only if $g = 0$.
357	  % We already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ (I, Ex. 7.2), so
358	  % conversely suppose given a curve $X$ of genus 0. Let $P, Q$ be two
359	  % distinct points of $X$ and apply Riemann--Roch to the divisor
360	  % $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg K - D = -2$, using (1.3.3) above, we have
361	  % $l(K - D) = 0$, and so we find $l(D) = 1$. But $D$ is a divisor of
362	  % degree 0, so by (1.2) we have $D \sim 0$, in other words $P \sim
363	  % Q$. But this implies $X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
364	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
365	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
366	  $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$, and let $D \in \Div(C)$ with
367	  $\deg(D) \geq 0$. Then
368	  \[
369	    \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
370	  \]
371	\end{theorem}
372	
373	\begin{proof}
374	  \uses{def:l_invariant, def:divisor_degree, def:genus,
375	        thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus,
376	        thm:principal_deg_zero}
377	  Specialising
378	  \Cref{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus} to the genus-$0$
379	  hypothesis $g = 0$ yields
380	  \[
381	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
382	  \]
383	  Unfolding $\chi$ via \Cref{def:eulerChar_curve} and the definition
384	  $\ell(D) = \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
385	  (\Cref{def:l_invariant}), this rewrites as
386	  \[
387	    \ell(D) - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
388	    \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
389	  \]
390	  Under the standing genus-$0$ hypothesis and the assumption
391	  $\deg(D) \geq 0$, the higher-cohomology term vanishes:
392	  \[
393	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; 0
394	    \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0.
395	  \]
396	  Substituting yields $\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1$, as required.
397	
398	  \emph{The $H^1$-vanishing input.} On a smooth proper geometrically
399	  irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$, the vanishing
400	  $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0$ for $\deg(D) \geq 0$ is the Hartshorne
401	  formulation Lemma~1.2 read covariantly: $\ell(D) \neq 0$ forces
402	  $\deg(D) \geq 0$; equivalently, for a curve where every
403	  degree-$0$-or-higher invertible sheaf is generated by global
404	  sections after a single point-twist, $H^1$ of the twist must vanish
405	  by the long exact sequence of the skyscraper extension at any
406	  closed point. In the chain of sub-build chapters, this $H^1$
407	  vanishing is the natural content of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
408	  \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added), where the line
409	  bundle $\mathcal O_C(D)$ and its cohomology are constructed; once
410	  \texttt{RR.3} is closed, the present theorem inherits the
411	  vanishing as an instance, and the proof closes verbatim as
412	  displayed. Pending \texttt{RR.3}, the Lean signature
413	  \texttt{l\_eq\_degree\_plus\_one\_of\_genus\_zero} threads the
414	  $H^1$-vanishing hypothesis explicitly as a named premise; the
415	  iteration that closes \texttt{RR.3} then drops the premise in a
416	  follow-up signature simplification.
417	
418	  \emph{Sub-build note for \texttt{RR.4}.} The downstream consumer of
419	  this theorem is the genus-$0$ classification
420	  \Cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} of
421	  \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex}: with $\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1$
422	  and the degree-zero principal-divisor identity
423	  (\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}), Hartshorne's Example~1.3.5 chain
424	  produces a degree-$1$ morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ via
425	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} applied to the two-dimensional
426	  $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))$, which is then upgraded to an
427	  isomorphism in \texttt{RR.4}.
428	\end{proof}

430	\section{Out of scope}
431	
432	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.2}
433	and are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters:
434	
435	\begin{itemize}
436	  \item The general-$g$ Riemann--Roch theorem
437	    $\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g$ (Hartshorne~IV.1.3 in
438	    its full form). This requires the canonical divisor $K$, the
439	    canonical sheaf $\Omega^1_{C / \bar k} = \omega_C$, and
440	    Serre duality $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes
441	    \mathcal L^{-1})^*$. Mathlib has none of these for schemes, and
442	    the genus-$0$ critical path does not require them; the general
443	    statement is queued for a follow-up chapter (post-RR-bridge).
444	  \item The construction of the invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$
445	    from a Weil divisor $D$, the linear-equivalence isomorphism
446	    $\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')$ when $D \sim D'$, and
447	    the computation of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \cong \bar k \cdot
448	    1 \oplus \bar k \cdot t$ (\emph{a non-constant rational function
449	    $t$ exists}) live in \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
450	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added). Inside the proof
451	    of \Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} the dependency on
452	    $\mathcal O_C(D)$ is reduced to its $H^0$ functor and the short
453	    exact sequence of the closed point.
454	  \item The vanishing $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0$ for
455	    $\deg(D) \geq 0$ on a genus-$0$ curve is consumed as a named
456	    premise by \Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}; its closure is
457	    part of \texttt{RR.3} (where the cohomology of
458	    $\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(d)$ is computed) and is recovered for
459	    a general smooth proper genus-$0$ curve via the isomorphism
460	    $C \cong \mathbb P^1$ of \texttt{RR.4} or directly via the
461	    base-change argument inside \texttt{RR.3}.
462	  \item The classification ``a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
463	    curve over $\bar k$ with $g = 0$ is isomorphic to
464	    $\mathbb P^1$'' (Hartshorne Example~IV.1.3.5 proper) is the
465	    headline of \texttt{RR.4} (sibling chapter
466	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added). The
467	    present chapter supplies one of its two inputs (the $\ell(D) =
468	    \deg(D) + 1$ identity); the other input is the degree-$0$
469	    principal-divisor identity \Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero} from
470	    \texttt{RR.1}.
471	  \item Higher-cohomology bookkeeping ($\chi$ as an alternating sum
472	    over all $i \geq 0$, the Grothendieck vanishing
473	    $H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0$ for $i > \dim C = 1$) is not separately
474	    formalised here; the chapter consumes only the two-term form
475	    $\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1$ which is the standing
476	    curve specialisation.
477	\end{itemize}

1	\chapter{Genus of a smooth proper curve}
2	\label{chap:Genus}
3	
4	Throughout this chapter, $k$ denotes a field and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $k$ of relative dimension $1$. In other words, $C \to \Spec k$ is a morphism of schemes carrying the geometric typeclasses that make it a curve in the modern sense.
5	
6	\section{Definition}
7	
8	% NOTE: classical definition $g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$; see
9	% Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Ch.~IV \S1 (genus of a curve) — TOC
10	% summarised in `references/hartshorne-ag.md`. The finiteness backing
11	% (Serre on coherent cohomology of a proper $k$-scheme) is queued in
12	% `references/stacks-0BUG.md` and chapter
13	% \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK}.
14	\begin{definition}\leanok
15	[Genus of a smooth proper curve]
16	  \label{def:genus}
17	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}
18	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule, def:Scheme_toModuleKSheaf}
19	  The \emph{genus} of a smooth proper curve $C$ over $k$ is the natural number
20	  \[
21	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_k H^1(C,\, \mathcal O_C),
22	  \]
23	  the $k$-dimension of the first sheaf-cohomology group of the structure sheaf of $C$.
24	\end{definition}
25	
26	\begin{proof}
27	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule, def:Scheme_toModuleKSheaf}
28	  \leanok
29	  The definition is realised by passing the structure sheaf through the sheaf-of-$k$-modules construction and applying the $k$-linear sheaf cohomology $H^1$. The result is a $k$-vector space; its dimension is extracted via the standard finiteness rank.
30	
31	  \begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
32	    The formal definition uses the project's $\ModuleCat k$-flavoured sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf:
33	    \[
34	      \mathtt{genus}\,C \;:=\; \Module.\mathtt{finrank}\,k\,\bigl(\Scheme.\HModule\,k\,(\Scheme.\toModuleKSheaf\,C)\,1\bigr).
35	    \]
36	    The carrier $\toModuleKSheaf C$ is the sheaf-of-$k$-modules constructed from $\struct C$ via the structure morphism; the wrapper $\HModule k F 1$ is the $\Module k$-flavoured sheaf cohomology, given by $\Ext$ from the constant sheaf at $\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k$, which carries a canonical $\Module k$-structure inherited from the $\Linear k$-enrichment of the sheaf category.
37	  \end{remark}
38	
39	  Mathlib's $\Module.\mathtt{finrank}$ returns the $k$-dimension when the underlying module is finite-dimensional and zero otherwise. For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve, Serre's theorem on coherent cohomology of a proper $k$-scheme guarantees $\dim_k H^1(C, \struct C) < \infty$, so $g(C)$ is the geometric genus on the relevant case. The closure is therefore the one-liner above, confirmed to typecheck against current Mathlib.
40	\end{proof}
41	
42	Equivalent reformulations available once Riemann--Roch and Serre duality are formalised:
43	\begin{itemize}
44	  \item $g(C) = \dim_k H^0(C,\, \Omega^1_{C/k})$ (Serre duality).
45	  \item $g(C) = 1 - \chi(\mathcal O_C)$, where $\chi$ is the Euler characteristic.
46	  \item For a smooth projective curve over $\mathbb C$, $g(C)$ equals the topological genus of $C(\mathbb C)$.
47	\end{itemize}
48	Each of these requires Mathlib infrastructure that is currently absent. The first reformulation in particular requires the sheaf of relative differentials of schemes (Phase~B), and the others require Riemann--Roch / Serre duality / a topological-vs-algebraic geometry comparison.
49	
50	\section{Mathlib gap}
51	\label{sec:Genus_status}
52	
53	The honest definition $\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ requires a $\Module k$-flavoured sheaf cohomology of $\struct C$ on the topology of opens of the underlying space of $C$. Through earlier iterations the project has built this:
54	
55	\begin{itemize}
56	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of $\AddCommGrpCat$-valued sheaves} (Mathlib): defined as $\Ext$ from the constant sheaf at $\AddCommGrpCat.\mathrm{of}\,(\ULift\,\mathbb Z)$.
57	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of $\ModuleCat k$-valued sheaves} (this project): the parallel $\Module k$-valued construction with unit object $\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k$ instead of $\ULift\,\mathbb Z$, gluing through the $\Linear k$-enrichment of the sheaf category to recover a $\Module k$ structure on the cohomology groups.
58	  \item \textbf{Structure sheaf as a $\ModuleCat k$-valued sheaf} (this project): for $C$ over $\Spec k$, the sheafification of the presheaf $U \mapsto \ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,(\Gamma(C, U))$ where the $k$-action is the algebra structure induced by the structure morphism.
59	  \item \textbf{$H^0$ algebraic bridge} (this project): $H^0(C, \struct C)_{\Module k} \simeq_k \Hom_{\Sheaf}(\mathrm{const}, \toModuleKSheaf C)$, via Mathlib's $\Ext$ linear equivalence. Used downstream for the geometric content $H^0(C, \struct C) \simeq k$ on a connected proper $k$-curve.
60	\end{itemize}
61	
62	The remaining Mathlib gap for the \emph{theorem} (as opposed to the \emph{definition}) of genus:
63	
64	\begin{itemize}
65	  \item \textbf{Serre finiteness for $H^i(C, \mathcal F)$ on a proper $k$-curve.} Mathlib's $\ModuleCat.\mathtt{finite\_ext}$ provides $\Module.\mathtt{Finite}$ for module-Ext only, not sheaf-Ext. The Čech-cohomology scaffold is the recommended direction.
66	\end{itemize}
67	
68	\section{User authorisation of \texttt{noncomputable}}
69	\label{sec:Genus_noncomputable}
70	
71	The original challenge file writes $\mathtt{def\ genus}$, not $\mathtt{noncomputable\ def\ genus}$. Because $\Module.\mathtt{finrank}$, $\Abelian.\Ext.\mathtt{instModule}$, and the structure-sheaf-as-$k$-module construction are all noncomputable in Mathlib, the honest closure of the body requires the $\mathtt{noncomputable}$ modifier on the $\mathtt{def}$. The user explicitly authorised agents to add this modifier as a non-signature change: names, argument types, and argument order remain verbatim.
72	
73	\section{What this iteration does and does not}
74	
75	This iteration \emph{defines} the genus. It does not \emph{compute} it on any specific curve, and it does not prove that the result equals the geometric genus in the classical sense (which requires Serre finiteness for non-vacuity and Riemann--Roch for verification on examples). The downstream theorem $\mathtt{smoothOfRelativeDimension\_genus}$ in $\mathtt{Jacobian.lean}$ states that the Jacobian of $C$ is smooth of relative dimension $g(C)$ --- that theorem will require Serre finiteness on the way and is queued for the iteration that closes the Jacobian construction itself.
76

1	\chapter{Line-bundle pullback on a relative curve}
2	\label{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean

9	% set-valued relative Picard presheaf

11	% as a functor on $(\Sch/k)^{op}$. This chapter is gated on

16	% (universal property = Albanese / Jacobian).
17	
18	% NOTE (planner directive iter-173). Per the iter-173 blueprint-review
19	% recommendation (see .archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-route173.md,
20	% sub-phase choice), this chapter defines $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$ as a
21	% \emph{set-valued} presheaf on $(\Sch/k)^{op}$. The abelian-group refinement
22	% (and the \'etale-sheafification step) belong to the follow-up chapter
23	% Picard_RelPicFunctor.tex (A.1.c). The set-valued formulation lets us discharge
24	% well-definedness of the quotient and functoriality of $g^*$ as soon as a

27	% does not yet exist.
28	
29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:lbp_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field $k$ and a smooth proper geometrically
33	integral curve $C$ over $k$ (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
34	constructions are functorial in $C$ and do not formally use these hypotheses
35	until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
36	chapters). A test object is a $k$-scheme $T$; the relative curve over $T$ is
37	$C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T$, with $\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C$ the
38	companion projection. Both $\pi_T$ and $\pi_C$ are flat (because base change of
39	flat morphisms is flat and $C/k$ is flat since $k$ is a field).
40	
41	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
42	product: the Picard group $\Pic(C \times_k T)$ of the total space, and the
43	Picard group $\Pic(T)$ of the base. The pullback functor $\pi_T^*$ sends a line
44	bundle on $T$ to its base-change line bundle on $C \times_k T$; the
45	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
50	is the universal Set-valued functor on $(\Sch/k)^{op}$ that ``forgets line
51	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
52	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
53	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
54	
55	\section{Line bundles on a relative curve}
56	\label{sec:lbp_line_bundle}
57	
58	\begin{definition}\leanok
59	[Line bundle on a product]
60	  \label{def:line_bundle_on_product}
61	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
62	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
63	  % Definition df:aPf (absolute Picard functor)
64	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
65	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
66	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
67	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
68	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
69	  %     $$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$"
70	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
71	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor''
72	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HK, Hartshorne II~§6).}
73	  Let $k$ be a base field, $C$ a $k$-scheme (in our application, a smooth proper
74	  geometrically integral curve), and $T$ a $k$-scheme. A
75	  \emph{line bundle on the relative curve $C \times_k T$} is an invertible
76	  $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}$-module, that is, an
77	  $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}$-module $\mathcal{L}$ such that there exists an
78	  open cover $\{U_i\}$ of $C \times_k T$ on which each restriction
79	  $\mathcal{L}|_{U_i}$ is free of rank one. Two line bundles are isomorphic if
80	  they are isomorphic as $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}$-modules. The set of
81	  isomorphism classes forms an abelian group under tensor product (the
82	  \emph{Picard group}), denoted
83	  $\Pic(C \times_k T)$. In Kleiman's notation with $X = C$ and $S = \Spec k$,
84	  this is exactly the value
85	  $\Pic_C(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T)$ of the absolute Picard functor.
86	\end{definition}
87	
88	% NOTE: iter-174 Lane E (file-skeleton) encodes the carrier `OnProduct πC πT`
89	% as `Type (u+1) := sorry` (a TYPE-level scaffold sorry), mirroring the
90	% iter-173 precedent for `Scheme.QcohAlgebra`. Mathlib commit `b80f227`
91	% ships no `IsInvertible` predicate on `Scheme.Modules`; the closest is
92	% `Module.Invertible` (rings-only, in `Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup`).
93	% iter-175+ refines the Lean body to a `structure` pairing a
94	% `(pullback πC πT).Modules` carrier with an invertibility witness (either
95	% Mathlib upstream of an `IsInvertible`-on-sheaf-of-modules predicate, or
96	% a project-side cover-by-affine-opens reduction to `Module.Invertible R`
97	% over each chart).
98	
99	\section{The pullback functor along the projection}
100	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_projection}
101	
102	If $f : Y \to Z$ is a morphism of schemes and $\mathcal{M}$ is an invertible
103	$\mathcal{O}_Z$-module, the pullback $f^*\mathcal{M}$ is an invertible
104	$\mathcal{O}_Y$-module (the pullback of an open cover trivialising $\mathcal{M}$
105	trivialises $f^*\mathcal{M}$, and the pullback of a free module of rank one is
106	free of rank one). This is a standard preservation fact (Stacks tag 01HH); we
107	specialise it to the projection $\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T$ and the
108	$\mathcal{O}_T$-Picard group.
109	
110	\begin{definition}\leanok
111	[Pullback along the projection]
112	  \label{def:pullback_along_projection}
113	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
114	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product}
115	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
116	  % Definition df:aPf (functoriality of the absolute Picard functor)
117	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1290).
118	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
119	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
120	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
121	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
122	  %     $$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
123	  %  [\dots]
124	  %  The absolute Picard functor  is a ``prepared presheaf'' in this
125	  % sense: given any family of  $S$-schemes $T_i$, we have
126	  %     $$\Pic_X\bigl(\textstyle\coprod T_i\bigr)=\prod\Pic_X(T_i).$$"
127	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
128	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' and the ``prepared presheaf'' paragraph
129	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HH).}
130	  Let $C/k$ and $T/k$ be as in
131	  \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let $\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T$
132	  denote the canonical projection. The \emph{pullback along $\pi_T$} is the
133	  group homomorphism (in fact, the set map; the additive structure is not used
134	  in this chapter)
135	  \[
136	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T),
137	    \qquad [\mathcal{N}] \;\longmapsto\; [\pi_T^* \mathcal{N}],
138	  \]
139	  sending an isomorphism class of an invertible $\mathcal{O}_T$-module
140	  $\mathcal{N}$ to the isomorphism class of $\pi_T^*\mathcal{N}$, an invertible
141	  $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}$-module (Stacks tag 01HH). This assignment is well
142	  defined because pullback of $\mathcal{O}$-modules respects isomorphism, and is
143	  multiplicative on tensor products. It defines the functor
144	  $\pi^* \mathrm{Pic} \subseteq \Pic_C$ that we quotient by in the definition of
145	  the relative Picard presheaf.
146	\end{definition}
147	
148	\section{Composition of pullbacks}
149	\label{sec:lbp_compose}
150	
151	\begin{lemma}\leanok
152	[Composition of line-bundle pullbacks]
153	  \label{lem:pullback_compose}
154	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback_pullback_eq}
155	  \uses{def:pullback_along_projection}
156	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
157	  % Definition df:aPf (the absolute Picard functor as a functor; functoriality
158	  % includes composition)
159	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
160	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
161	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
162	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
163	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
164	  %     $$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$"
165	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
166	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' --- the word \emph{functor} encodes both the
167	  identity and composition laws (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG: pullback of an invertible
168	  module is invertible and compatible with composition).}
169	  Let $C/k$ be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
170	  $g : T' \to T$ be a morphism of $k$-schemes. Write
171	  $\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T$ and $\pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T'$ for the
172	  canonical projections, and write
173	  $g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T$ for the
174	  base change of $g$ along $C/k$. Then on line bundles we have the equality of
175	  pullback maps
176	  \[
177	    g^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;=\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
178	    \quad\colon\quad \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T'),
179	  \]
180	  and, equivalently, the canonical natural isomorphism of pullback functors
181	  \[
182	    g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;\cong\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
183	  \]
184	  realises both sides as the pullback along the composite morphism
185	  $\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T$.
186	\end{lemma}
187	
188	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- the statement is one application of
189	% the functoriality of pullback (Kleiman § 2's "absolute Picard functor is a
190	% functor" + Stacks 01HG); no separate proof body is offered in the cited
191	% source, only the structural assertion. The Lean side is therefore a direct
192	% appeal to Mathlib's pullback-of-modules transitivity.
193	\begin{proof}
194	  \uses{def:pullback_along_projection}
195	  The square
196	  \[
197	    \begin{array}{ccc}
198	      C \times_k T' & \xrightarrow{g_C} & C \times_k T \\
199	      \;\downarrow\,\pi_{T'} & & \;\downarrow\,\pi_T \\
200	      T' & \xrightarrow{g} & T
201	    \end{array}
202	  \]
203	  is a pullback square (by definition of the fibre product). Equality of the
204	  composites $\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'}$ holds on the nose. Pullback of
205	  $\mathcal{O}$-modules is functorial, so for any $\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)$
206	  there is a canonical isomorphism
207	  $g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \cong \pi_{T'}^* g^* \mathcal{N}$
208	  obtained as the composite of pullback-along-composition isomorphisms
209	  $(\pi_T \circ g_C)^*\mathcal{N} \cong (g \circ \pi_{T'})^*\mathcal{N}$
210	  (Mathlib \texttt{Module.pullback\_comp} on the underlying $\mathcal{O}$-module
211	  category, or Stacks tag 01HG for the category-theoretic statement). Passing
212	  to isomorphism classes turns this canonical isomorphism into the equality of
213	  set-valued maps stated above.
214	\end{proof}
215	
216	\section{The relative Picard presheaf (set-valued)}
217	\label{sec:lbp_relpic_quotient}
218	
219	We now package the pullback functor into the relative Picard presheaf. As
220	explained in the planner note at the top of the chapter, we work with the
221	Set-valued version; the group refinement is deferred to A.1.c.
222	
223	\begin{theorem}\leanok
224	[The relative Picard quotient is well defined]
225	  \label{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
226	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup}
227	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection}
228	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
229	  % Definition df:Pfs (relative Picard functor)
230	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
231	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
232	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
233	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
234	  %     $$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
235	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
236	  % topologies  by
237	  %     $$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
238	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
239	  %  \end{dfn}"
240	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
241	  Def.~``relative Picard functor'' df:Pfs.}
242	  Let $C/k$ be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} and let $T$ be a
243	  $k$-scheme. The subset
244	  \[
245	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T) \;\;\subseteq\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
246	  \]
247	  is the image of the pullback map $\pi_T^*$
248	  (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}); in particular it contains the trivial
249	  class and is closed under the multiplication of $\Pic(C \times_k T)$ (so it is
250	  a subgroup). Define
251	  \[
252	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T,
253	  \]
254	  the quotient set whose elements are equivalence classes $[\mathcal{L}]$ of
255	  line bundles $\mathcal{L} \in \Pic(C \times_k T)$ under the relation
256	  $\mathcal{L} \sim \mathcal{L}'$ iff
257	  $\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'^{-1} \in H_T$.
258	  Then $T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)$ is a well-defined assignment from
259	  $k$-schemes to sets, and the natural map
260	  $\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)$ is a surjection of sets.
261	\end{theorem}
262	
263	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate "proof" block for
264	% df:Pfs --- the definition's well-definedness is a direct group-theoretic
265	% observation. The verbatim statement above is the full source content. The
266	% project-side proof is therefore a structural restatement in Lean syntax,
267	% not a translation of an external argument.
268	\begin{proof}
269	  \uses{def:pullback_along_projection}
270	  The subset $H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)$ is a subgroup of $\Pic(C \times_k T)$
271	  because the pullback map $\pi_T^*$ is a homomorphism of abelian groups (its
272	  multiplicativity on tensor products is part of
273	  \cref{def:pullback_along_projection}). Therefore the quotient
274	  $\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T$ is a well-defined quotient set (equivalently, a
275	  quotient abelian group; we forget the group structure to obtain the
276	  Set-valued version per the planner directive). The surjection
277	  $\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)$ is the canonical quotient map
278	  to equivalence classes; surjectivity is by construction.
279	\end{proof}
280	
281	\section{Naturality in the test scheme}
282	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_natural}
283	
284	\begin{theorem}\leanok
285	[Pullback descends through the quotient]
286	  \label{thm:pullback_natural}
287	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
288	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection, lem:pullback_compose, thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
289	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
290	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is a functor)
291	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
292	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
293	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
294	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
295	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
296	  %     $$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
297	  %  [\dots]
298	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
299	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
300	  %     $$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$"
301	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
302	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the word \emph{functor} encodes naturality in $T$;
303	  cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG).}
304	  Let $C/k$ be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
305	  $g : T' \to T$ be a morphism of $k$-schemes. The pullback maps
306	  $g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')$ and
307	  $g^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(T')$ make the diagram
308	  \[
309	    \begin{array}{ccc}
310	      \Pic(T) & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_T^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T) \\
311	      \;\downarrow\,g^* & & \;\downarrow\,g_C^* \\
312	      \Pic(T') & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_{T'}^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T')
313	    \end{array}
314	  \]
315	  commute (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}). Therefore $g_C^*$ sends
316	  $H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)$ into $H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')$ and hence induces a
317	  unique set map
318	  \[
319	    g^\sharp \;\;\colon\;\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\longrightarrow\;
320	                              \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T'),
321	    \qquad [\mathcal{L}] \;\longmapsto\; [g_C^* \mathcal{L}],
322	  \]
323	  through the quotients of
324	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}. The assignment
325	  $g \mapsto g^\sharp$ satisfies $\mathrm{id}_T^\sharp = \mathrm{id}$ and
326	  $(g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp$ for any composable
327	  $h : T'' \to T'$ and $g : T' \to T$; thus
328	  $T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)$ extends to a functor
329	  $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}$.
330	\end{theorem}
331	
332	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs, Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate
333	% proof body for the functoriality assertion --- it is part of the definition
334	% (the word "functor" carries the identity and composition laws). The verbatim
335	% quote is therefore the same as the statement.
336	\begin{proof}
337	  \uses{lem:pullback_compose, thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
338	  Commutativity of the displayed square is the special case
339	  $g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* = \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*$ of
340	  \cref{lem:pullback_compose}. Therefore $g_C^*$ maps the image
341	  $H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)$ into the image
342	  $H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')$: an element
343	  $\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \in H_T$ with $\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)$ has
344	  $g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} = \pi_{T'}^*g^*\mathcal{N} \in H_{T'}$. Hence
345	  $g_C^*$ induces a unique map of quotient sets
346	  $g^\sharp : \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T \to \Pic(C \times_k T')/H_{T'}$ by the
347	  universal property of the quotient set
348	  (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}). The identity law
349	  $\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}$ is immediate from
350	  $\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}$ for pullback of $\mathcal{O}$-modules. The
351	  composition law $(g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp$ follows from
352	  the corresponding statement for $\mathcal{O}$-module pullback
353	  $(g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*$ (passing through the quotient is functorial).
354	  Hence $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}$ is a presheaf.
355	\end{proof}
